retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 11 (Accessory) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Minor injury can occur with daily movements or behaviors that exceed the elastic capacity of the SAN, such as excessive stretching or carrying heavy weights. However, the nerve may be severely damaged during neck tumor mass removal, particularly when the blood supply is compromised or partial resection is performed. In certain cases, removal of this nerve is accomplished to compensate for a peripheral nerve lesion, transfer it where electrical activity is absent, and attempt to restore limb function. [1] [2] Understanding the anatomy and function of CN XI allows clinicians to prevent iatrogenic injury, accurately localize lesions, and implement targeted rehabilitation strategies to preserve or restore motor function.

retrieved_docs.title: Accessory Nerve Injury -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: As the accessory nerve travels down and away from the brain, the cranial and spinal pieces of the nerve come together to form the spinal accessory nerve (SAN). The fusion of cranial and spinal contributions within the skull base forms the spinal accessory nerve. It exits the skull through the jugular foramen adjacent to the vagus nerve. [2] The spinal accessory nerve descends alongside the internal jugular vein, coursing posterior to the styloid process, posterior belly of the digastric muscle, and sternocleidomastoid muscle (SCM) before entering the posterior cervical triangle (see Image. Superficial Neck Anatomy). The accessory nerve leaves the jugular foramen along with the glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX) and vagus nerve (CN X) (see Image. Dural Venous Sinuses). It travels to the SCM, either superficial or deep, and then enters the trapezius muscle, where a major accessory nerve trunk converges with C2, C3, or both. The traveling pathway of this nerve provides functional significance to the structures in the posterior neck. However, due to its long and superficial nature, the accessory nerve is prone to injury. Injury to the accessory nerve could be from blunt trauma, incidental, or, most commonly, iatrogenic reasons. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Accessory Nerve Injury -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The cranial nerves are twelve pairs that travel outside the skull via foramina to innervate various structures. Cranial nerve (CN) XI is also known as the accessory nerve (see Image. The Accessory Nerve). According to the morphology of the cranial root of the accessory nerve, it is made of the union of 2 to 4 short filaments, making the cranial roots of the accessory nerve. Two to 9 rootlets join the spinal root to form the nerve. The cranial roots of CN XI could be considered part of the vagus nerve when factoring in the function of the 2 nerves. Both the cranial roots of the accessory nerve and the vagus nerve originate from the nucleus ambiguus and dorsal nucleus of the vagus nerve and travel to the laryngeal muscles, supplying the motor fibers (see Image. Muscles of the Head, Face, and Neck). [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 11 (Accessory) -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Trauma to the upper cervical region or poor posture can similarly impair nerve function. The number of myelinated fibers in CN XI ranges from 1,700 to 2,000, and the nerve diameter is approximately 2 mm. Recent cadaveric studies have confirmed these anatomical observations. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 11 (Accessory) -- Surgical Considerations -- Nerve Transfer
retrieved_docs.content: CN XI may be used in neurotization and reinnervation procedures involving cervical plexus target nerves, such as the radial, suprascapular, musculocutaneous, and axillary nerves. In patients with quadriplegia, CN XI may be transferred to replace the phrenic nerve. Conversely, when CN XI is permanently damaged, reinnervation may be achieved using the upper trunk of the brachial plexus, particularly fascicles destined for the axillary nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Cranial Nerve Testing -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Cranial Nerve XI
retrieved_docs.content: Central root or nuclear damage to the spinal accessory nerve results in ipsilateral flaccid paralysis of the sternocleidomastoid (with difficulty turning the head against force) and partial ipsilateral trapezius paralysis leading to shoulder drop. The trapezius is innervated by anterior horn grey matter from cervical spinal regions C3 through C4/5 in addition to the spinal accessory nerve. Thus, complete paralysis of the trapezius muscle will not occur following a simple focal lesion.

retrieved_docs.title: Accessory Nerve Injury -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Various factors influence spinal accessory nerve (SAN) function after an insult, such as the type of neck dissection, the extent of injury, radiation therapy, the interval between trauma and repair, the vascularity, and the length of the graft. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 11 (Accessory) -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The SAN anastomoses with the root of C1, though some authors report connections with C2 to C3. Other linkages include the stellate ganglion and glossopharyngeal nerve, whose functions have not been established. A branch of the great auricular nerve communicates with CN XI near the jugular foramen. At the upper border of the transverse process of the C1 vertebra, CN XI joins CN X via the Lobstein anastomosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 11 (Accessory) -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Once released from the dura, CN XI divides into internal and external branches. The internal branch contains fibers from the cranial portion, forming an anastomosis with the vagus nerve at the upper border of the transverse process of the 1st cervical vertebra (C1) and contributing parasympathetic fibers to the vagal innervation of the pharyngeal and laryngeal regions (see Image . Course of the Vagus Nerve). The external branch consists mainly of fibers from the spinal portion, which descend to innervate the trapezius and SCM (see Image . Hypoglossal, Vagus, and Glossopharyngeal Nerves in the Neck). These muscles also receive motor impulses from cervical nerves C1 to C4.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma Neurological Exam -- Technique or Treatment -- Cranial Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The accessory nerve (CN XI) may be assessed in a responsive individual by asking them to shrug their shoulders upward or rotating the head side to side against resistance. Normal tongue movement and strength and absence of lateral deviation are signs of an intact hypoglossal nerve (CN XII).

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 0 (Terminal Nerve) -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The anatomical positioning of CN0 poses important clinical considerations, especially for otorhinolaryngology surgical procedures. Given its potential neuroreproductive role, damage or lacerations to CN0 during routine otolaryngological surgical procedures may compromise its structural integrity and potential functionality. In animal models, the laceration of the terminal nerve has been reported to cause GnRH deficiencies. However, there are no clinical studies to confirm these findings in humans.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 11 (Accessory) -- Surgical Considerations -- Iatrogenic Lesions
retrieved_docs.content: The course of CN XI makes it particularly vulnerable to iatrogenic injuries during procedures involving the posterior triangle of the neck. Palsy following internal jugular vein cannulation is an example. Carotid endarterectomy, cardiac or pulmonary surgery with sternotomy, radiation therapy, cervical lymph node biopsy, and thyroid surgery may produce similar healthcare-associated complications. [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Cranial Nerve Testing -- Clinical Significance -- Cranial Nerve XI
retrieved_docs.content: The approach to managing accessory nerve injury and resulting trapezius muscle deformity is an interprofessional approach that revolves around conservative care, physical therapy, and surgical treatment. Treatment and management depend on the severity and the cause of the spinal accessory nerve injury. Immediate therapy should be considered for severe presentations, such as penetrating traumas and iatrogenic injuries. [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Cranial Nerve Testing -- Technique or Treatment -- Cranial Nerve XI
retrieved_docs.content: Isolated sternocleidomastoid weakness indicates pathology within the brainstem tegmentum or upper cervical accessory root. Peripheral lesions can cause isolated weakness of either the sternocleidomastoid or the trapezius. Damage within the contralateral brainstem, ipsilateral high cervical cord, or an accessory nerve lesion peripherally before the nerve bifurcates to both muscles results in ipsilateral weakness of both the sternocleidomastoid and the trapezius. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve -- Structure and Function -- Cranial Nerve XI (Accessory Nerve)
retrieved_docs.content: Cranial nerve XI, the spinal accessory nerve, provides GSE motor innervation to the trapezius and sternocleidomastoid muscles via the spinal nucleus of the accessory nerve. This nucleus is located within the cervical spinal cord, spanning from the levels of C1 to approximately C5 or C6. The fibers emerge as independent roots, separate from the anterior or dorsal spinal roots of the central spinal grey matter, and ascend through the foramen magnum to enter the cranial cavity. From there, these fibers exit the cranial cavity via the jugular foramen alongside cranial nerves IX and X.

retrieved_docs.title: Accessory Nerve Injury -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: The 11th cranial nerve, also known as the accessory nerve, is prone to injury due to its long and superficial nature. Damage to the accessory nerve can be incidental, iatrogenic, or due to blunt trauma. This activity reviews the anatomy of the nerve and describes the evaluation and treatment of accessory nerve injury. It also highlights the role of interprofessional team members in managing this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: The central nervous system (centrální nervový systém) is described as a physical component of the dura mater, indicating its anatomical relationship within the meningeal coverings. In clinical assessment, the central nervous system serves as the finding site for central nervous system injury (إصابة الجهاز العصبي المركزي), highlighting its relevance as the location where such pathological findings are identified. Understanding that the central nervous system is both anatomically linked to the dura mater and is the primary site of CNS injuries informs diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in neurology and neurosurgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is classified as a clinical action within the broader group “Clinical actions A‑G” and serves as an umbrella term for a wide range of operative procedures. It includes adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with possible removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as several biliary surgeries such as cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, and associated cholangiography, which can be performed via open or laparoscopic techniques. Craniectomy or craniotomy performed exploratively in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region also falls under Excision, as do various parathyroidectomy approaches—including cervical, sternal‑split, and transthoracic routes—each potentially combined with mediastinal exploration. In spinal surgery, Excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc at a single lumbar level, but explicitly excludes laminectomy procedures that involve only exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for spinal stenosis across cervical and thoracic segments (both 1‑2 vertebral levels and more extensive segments). Additionally, Excision covers “other or therapeutic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx” and is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, highlighting its relevance to both general surgical and specialized reconstructive interventions. This comprehensive definition clarifies the scope of Excision, distinguishing it from specific laminectomy techniques while integrating diverse surgical modalities under a single procedural category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1619
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is described as a broad surgical approach that encompasses a variety of operative techniques used to remove tissue, explore anatomical regions, or treat disease. Specific procedures classified under the Excision method include partial or complete adrenalectomy with or without adrenal gland biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal routes; cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, with or without choledochoenterostomy; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial locations; suboccipital craniectomy aimed at cranial‑nerve exploration or decompression; radical orchiectomy for tumor removal that incorporates abdominal exploration; and parathyroidectomy procedures that involve mediastinal or cervical exploration, sometimes requiring a sternal split or transthoracic approach. In addition, Excision is linked to other therapeutic interventions on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, indicating that it

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712
retrieved_docs.content: Excision serves as a broad parent category for a variety of surgical actions, including the set of procedures labeled “Clinical actions A‑G.” Within this overarching category, many specific operative methods are classified as forms of excision: cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (both open and laparoscopic), choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage or calculus removal (with or without cholecystotomy and with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) that decompress nerve roots and may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc excision, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. At the same time, the excision category explicitly excludes several laminectomy techniques used for spinal stenosis: thoracic laminectomy with cord or cauda equina decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for one or two vertebral segments; and cervical, lumbar, or thoracic laminectomy for more than two vertebral segments performed under the same constraints. Excision is also linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, indicating that excisional techniques extend beyond deep organ surgery to include facial and upper aerodigestive tract interventions. This integrated view highlights excision as a versatile surgical concept that encompasses numerous specific operative methods while delineating clear boundaries for procedures it does not cover.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2756
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of organ‑specific and region‑specific procedures while explicitly excluding certain extensive spinal decompressions. Within this category, adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, and sometimes accompanied by removal of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—uses an excision method. Cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, either with cholangiography or with choledochoenterostomy, is also classified as an excision technique. Exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region falls under the same umbrella. In the spine, excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated intervertebral disc when performed at a single interspace in the cervical or lumbar spine. By contrast, more extensive laminectomy procedures that decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina over more than two vertebral segments—whether cervical, lumbar, or thoracic—are specifically listed as excluded methods for excision. Other procedures grouped under excision include nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision, parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, and parathyroidectomy that involves mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach. Excision is positioned within the higher‑level grouping “Clinical actions A‑G,” indicating that it is one of the clinical actions encompassed by that classification. Additionally, excision is linked to plastic procedures on the nose and is identified as a method within the broader set of “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx,” reflecting its relevance to reconstructive and therapeutic interventions in those anatomical regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1573
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures encompass a wide range of surgical techniques used to investigate, diagnose, or treat pathology in diverse anatomic regions. The category includes adrenal gland operations such as the partial or complete adrenalectomy or adrenal exploration performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches, which may also involve biopsy and, when indicated, excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Biliary exploration is represented by cholecystectomy with common duct investigation, either through cholangiography or choledochoenterostomy, and can be performed laparoscopically. Cranial exploration procedures comprise exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the supratentorial compartment and suboccipital craniectomy aimed at decompression of cranial nerves, as well as transcranial orbital exploration for lesion removal. Spinal exploration is addressed by multilevel laminectomies of the cervical, thoracic, or lumbar spine that allow decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without additional facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy, and by lumbar laminotomies (hemilaminectomies) that include nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc fragment excision in a single‑level re‑exploration. Parathyroid exploration procedures include parathyroidectomy with mediastinal access achieved through cervical, sternal‑split, or transthoracic approaches. All of these specific methods are organized under the broader parent category “Clinical actions A‑G,” which groups related exploratory surgical interventions for systematic clinical documentation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 437
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad surgical category within the “Clinical actions A‑G” hierarchy that encompasses operative techniques designed to investigate, decompress, or remove pathology across multiple anatomic regions. In endocrine surgery, partial or complete adrenalectomy—performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally—and adrenal gland exploration (with or without biopsy) may be combined with excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Hepatobiliary exploration includes cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration, cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, choledochotomy or choledochostomy with drainage, calculus removal, and optional cholecystotomy. Neurosurgical exploration comprises infratentorial (posterior‑fossa) and supratentorial craniectomy or craniotomy, suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression, and transcranial orbital exploration with lesion excision. Spinal exploration is achieved

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1879
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of operative techniques and is positioned within the larger group of Clinical actions A‑G. It encompasses arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from joints, multiple forms of cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (including choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy, and cholangiography), and cranial procedures such as exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region and suboccipital craniectomy for cranial‑nerve decompression. Excision also covers orbital exploration by a transcranial approach, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, foraminotomy, partial facetectomy, and disc excision, as well as nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision. Parathyroid surgery falls under this umbrella, including standard parathyroidectomy, parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration via sternal split or transthoracic approaches, and combined mediastinal‑exploration procedures. In addition to these methods, Excision is linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx and maintains a specific relationship with plastic procedures on the nose. Certain extensive spinal procedures—such as multisegment thoracic laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. Together, these relationships define Excision as a versatile operative concept that integrates numerous specialized techniques while delineating its scope relative to broader clinical actions and excluded methods.
